title: Graphs & Networks
description: Network modeling in Python: Adjacency Lists, Adjacency Matrices, and pathfinding traversals (Depth-First Search & Breadth-First Search).

sections.title: Adjacency List Representation
sections.description: Graphs consist of vertices (nodes) and edges (relations). In Python, an adjacency list is represented using a dictionary where keys are vertices and values are lists/sets of adjacent nodes.
sections.codeBlock:
# Graph Representation
graph = {
    'A': ['B', 'C'],
    'B': ['D', 'E'],
    'C': ['F'],
    'D': [],
    'E': ['F'],
    'F': []
}

print(graph['A'])      # ['B', 'C'] (Neighbors of A)

sections.title: Graph Traversals (DFS & BFS)
sections.description: DFS explores nodes by going deep along each branch before backtracking (using stack/recursion). BFS explores all neighbors at the current depth level before moving deeper (using queue).

details:
Graph Representations: Graphs are represented either by Adjacency Matrices (2D arrays indicating edge connections) or Adjacency Lists. Adjacency lists are highly space-efficient for sparse graphs (graphs where the number of edges is much less than V^2), making dictionary-of-lists representations standard in Python.

Pathfinding Algorithms: BFS guarantees finding the shortest path between two nodes in an unweighted graph, making it perfect for peer-to-peer connection mapping. DFS is utilized in cycle detection, topological sorting (e.g. build system dependencies), and finding strongly connected components.
